beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Namaste, Liam — in for Bear. Why do four equal-energy states split into three lines — and why does only one integral survive out of sixteen? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Why do four equal-energy states split into three lines — and why does only one integral survive out of sixteen??

beats.narration_text: Switch on an electric field across hydrogen and its four equal-energy n-equals-2 states split. One line goes up, one goes down by the same amount, and two states stay put — giving exactly three spectral lines, the middle one twice as bright.
Why three, not four? Because the field mixes two of the four states into lopsided clouds — one leaning with the field, one against. Those two move. The other two stay symmetric and stay put.
Why do four equal-energy states split into three lines — and why does only one integral survive out of sixteen?

props.headline: Why do four equal-energy states split into three lines — and…

card.headline:
4 states → 3 lines.
Symmetry kills 15 of 16 matrix elements.
card.dek: The linear Stark effect: degeneracy, selection rules, and lopsided clouds

beats.narration_text: The four n-equals-2 states — 2s, 2p-zero, 2p-plus-one, 2p-minus-one — all sit at minus 3.4 eV. No ordinary perturbation theory works here: the four levels are exactly degenerate and the energy denominator blows up.

The electric field perturbation is e times script-E times z. To find the first-order energy shifts, we must build the 4-by-4 matrix of z between all pairs of states and diagonalize it. That is 16 integrals.

Parity kills 15 of them. The z operator is odd; a matrix element between two states of the same parity integrates to zero. The 2s state is even, the 2p states are odd. Any element within the same parity block vanishes. Only the 2s-to-2p-zero element survives — it mixes even with odd.

Diagonalizing the surviving 2-by-2 block gives two new states: the sum and difference of 2s and 2p-zero, each shifted by plus or minus 3 times a-nought times e times script-E. The 2p-plus-one and 2p-minus-one states decouple entirely and stay unshifted.

The shifted states are lopsided: the sum state piles charge below the nucleus, the difference state piles it above. A lopsided charge distribution has a permanent electric dipole — that is why it shifts linearly with the field.

The ground state has no degenerate partner to mix with, so its charge stays symmetric. It shifts only as the square of the field — a much smaller quadratic Stark effect. Degeneracy is what makes the n-equals-2 shift linear.

Illustrative: at a field of 10 to the 5 volts per centimeter the shift is 3 times a-nought times e times script-E, about 16 millielectron-volts. The unshifted middle line contains two states — its intensity is exactly double that of each shifted line.

Four degenerate states. Parity kills 15 of 16 integrals. One surviving matrix element mixes 2s with 2p-zero into two lopsided clouds, shifted plus and minus by 3 a-nought e script-E. Two states stay symmetric, stay put. Three lines, middle twice as bright.

props.headline: Four degenerate states. Parity kills 15 of 16 integrals. One surviving…

card.headline:
E = E₂ ± 3a₀eε  (linear in ε).
2p±₁ unshifted: middle line × 2 intensity.
card.dek: Degeneracy + parity: one integral survives, two lopsided clouds shift, two symmetric states stay put.

It shifts only as the square of the field — a much smaller quadratic Stark effect. Degeneracy is what makes the n-equals-2 shift linear.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [the mechanism just described] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
